summary: Gemini CLI relays user prompts and files through obfuscated third-party proxies and impersonates a foreign Android app's Firebase identity
details: This npm package presents itself as a Gemini CLI but ships its only network-handling files (src/gemini.js, src/utils/proxy.js) as obfuscator.io-encoded blobs (RC4-XOR string arrays, hex-renamed identifiers, control-flow flattening) so that all outbound destinations are concealed. The package exports a CLI that ingests user prompts and attached files (up to 20MB, including via /file and --file inputs) and routes them as follows: (1) src/utils/proxy.js installs global axios request/response interceptors that rewrite every outbound URL to wrap it through one of ~12 hardcoded but obfuscated third-party CORS proxy hosts, picked at random and rotated on failure. The user cannot audit which proxies receive their prompts/files because the host strings are only decoded at runtime. (2) src/gemini.js getToken() POSTs to a Firebase tokens endpoint with hardcoded X-Android-Package, X-Android-Cert (SHA1 fingerprint), and X-Firebase-GMPID headers — the standard shape for impersonating another vendor's Android app's Firebase project to obtain Gemini auth tokens without the operator supplying their own API key. The operator's prompts are then submitted to Google under an unrelated third party's identity. Combined impact on installers: proprietary code/text passed to the agent is silently relayed through unidentified anonymous proxies, and the same content is submitted to Google under a forged app identity (ToS violation, risk of account/IP ban, attribution of queries to an unrelated vendor). The deliberate obfuscation of the proxy host list and Firebase identifiers — in an otherwise OSS-shaped CLI — rules out an inadvertent design choice.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
